timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Call of Jeremiah

sections.header: Verses 1-8 — "I Will Raise Up a Righteous Branch"

heb.paragraph: God will raise up "a righteous Branch (ṣemaḥ ṣaddîq) from David's line" (v.5). The term ṣemaḥ ("branch/shoot/sprout") becomes a messianic title used by Zechariah (3:8, 6:12) and echoed in Isaiah's "shoot from the stump of Jesse" (Isa 11:1). The Branch will "do what is just and right in the land" — the very standard that Josiah met and his successors failed. The name "The LORD Our Righteousness" (YHWH ṣidqēnû) puns on Zedekiah's name (ṣidqiyyāhû, "my righteousness is YHWH"), implying that only the true messianic king will fulfill what Zedekiah's name promised.

"Woe to the shepherds (rōʿîm) who are destroying and scattering the sheep of my pasture" (v.1). The shepherd metaphor for kingship is ancient Near Eastern — the king as shepherd of the people. These shepherds have scattered the flock rather than gathering it. God will replace them with shepherds who genuinely care for the sheep (v.4), culminating in the supreme Shepherd-King, the Righteous Branch.

panels.ctx: Chapter 23 resolves the royal crisis of chapter 22. The failed human shepherds (kings) will be replaced by divinely appointed shepherds, and ultimately by the Righteous Branch — the messianic king from David's line. The oracle in vv.5-6 is one of the most important messianic prophecies in the Hebrew Bible, combining Davidic lineage, righteous rule, and the name "The LORD Our Righteousness." The new exodus promise from 16:14-15 is repeated in vv.7-8, framing the messianic hope within redemptive history.

cross.note: The "shoot from the stump of Jesse" — Isaiah's parallel messianic oracle with similar imagery of justice and righteousness characterizing the coming king.
God will set up one shepherd, "my servant David" — Ezekiel's parallel vision of the messianic shepherd-king.
Jesus declares "I am the good shepherd" — claiming the messianic shepherd role that Jeremiah and Ezekiel prophesied.
"Here is the man whose name is the Branch" — Zechariah develops the ṣemaḥ title into a full messianic expectation.

notes.note: "Woe to the shepherds who destroy and scatter!" — the indictment names the kings as the primary cause of national catastrophe. The shepherds will be punished for "the evil they have done."
God himself will gather the remnant and appoint true shepherds. The people will "no longer be afraid or terrified, nor will any be missing." Every lost sheep accounted for.

The Righteous Branch from David's line: he will "reign wisely and do what is just and right." His name — "The LORD Our Righteousness" — identifies the king's righteousness as derivative from God. True royal authority comes from divine endowment, not human achievement.

The new exodus oracle (repeated from 16:14-15) frames the messianic promise within the larger pattern of divine redemption. The Branch's reign inaugurates a restoration greater than the original exodus.

Calvin identifies the Righteous Branch as Christ: "No mere human king could bear the name The LORD Our Righteousness. Only the Messiah, who is both God and man, fulfills this prophecy." The pun on Zedekiah's name sharpens the contrast: the counterfeit and the genuine.
The term ṣemaḥ ("branch") derives from the root ṣmḥ ("to sprout/grow"). It suggests organic growth from a root — the messianic king emerges from the Davidic line as naturally as a branch from a tree.
The name YHWH ṣidqēnû ("The LORD Our Righteousness") is a deliberate wordplay on King Zedekiah's name (ṣidqiyyāhû). The true king will embody what Zedekiah's name merely claimed.

Lundbom identifies this oracle as the theological climax of the royal collection (chs. 21-23). The failed shepherds (ch. 22) are replaced by the Righteous Branch — the Davidic line is not abolished but purified.

Brueggemann reads the Righteous Branch oracle as "prophetic imagination at its finest." In the midst of total institutional failure, the prophet imagines a radically different future — not reform of the existing system but replacement by a divinely authorized alternative.

heb.paragraph: God contrasts prophetic dreams with his word: "Let the prophet who has a dream recount the dream, but let the one who has my word speak it faithfully. For what has straw to do with grain?" (v.28). The dream/word contrast distinguishes subjective experience from divine revelation. Dreams are "straw" — lightweight, insubstantial. The word of the LORD is "grain" — solid, nourishing, weighty.

The wordplay on maśśāʾ (vv.33-40) exploits the double meaning of the term: "oracle/burden." When the people ask "What is the oracle (maśśāʾ) of the LORD?" Jeremiah answers: "You are the burden (maśśāʾ)!" The word itself becomes the judgment — God will "cast off" (nāṭaš) the people who treated his word as a light thing.

panels.ctx: The anti-false-prophet polemic (vv.9-40) is the most extensive in the Hebrew Bible. It catalogs the prophets' sins: adultery, lying, strengthening the hands of evildoers, prophesying by Baal, leading the people astray. The Samaria prophets were bad enough (prophesying by Baal); the Jerusalem prophets are worse — they commit adultery and live in falsehood (v.14). The section climaxes with the fire/hammer/grain metaphors: God's word is not a gentle suggestion but an irresistible force.

cross.note: The Deuteronomic test for false prophets: even accurate predictions are invalid if they lead to worship of other gods.
Jesus warns of "false prophets who come to you in sheep's clothing" — the same concern with prophetic imposters.
"False prophets appeared among the people" — the NT continuation of Jeremiah's warning about those who exploit God's name for personal gain.

notes.note: Jeremiah's heart is broken; his bones tremble. "Both prophet and priest are godless; even in my temple I find their wickedness." The corruption has invaded the sanctuary itself.

Jerusalem's prophets are worse than Samaria's: "They commit adultery and live a lie. They strengthen the hands of evildoers, so that not one of them turns from their wickedness." Jerusalem has become Sodom and Gomorrah.

"Am I only a God nearby and not a God far away? Can anyone hide in secret places so that I cannot see them?" — divine omnipresence destroys the false prophets' assumption that their deceptions go unnoticed.

"Is not my word like fire and like a hammer that breaks a rock in pieces?" — the word of God is not gentle advice but irresistible force. Fire consumes; the hammer shatters. The false prophets' words are straw; God's word is fire and iron.
Calvin's classic comment: "The word of God is a hammer, not a feather. It does not caress the conscience but breaks it. Those who preach only comfort have replaced the hammer with a cushion."

"Who has stood in the council (sôd) of the LORD?" — the sôd is the divine assembly where God's decrees are issued. True prophets have access to this heavenly council; false prophets speak from their own imagination.
The maśśāʾ wordplay is untranslatable: "What is the burden of the LORD? You are the burden!" The punning answer turns the question back on the questioners.

Lundbom identifies this as the "false prophecy collection" — the most comprehensive treatment of the prophet-vs-prophet conflict in the Hebrew Bible. The section establishes criteria for discerning true from false prophecy: access to the divine council (v.18), moral integrity (v.14), and the fruit of the message (v.22).

Brueggemann reads the straw/grain and fire/hammer contrasts as a defense of prophetic disruption. Comfortable religion is straw — lightweight and easily consumed. True prophecy is grain that nourishes and fire that transforms. The church must decide which it prefers.

themes.note: Prophecy peaks at 10 — the most extensive false prophecy polemic in the OT. The Righteous Branch lifts Mercy to 6 and Mission to 7, and Justice remains at 8.

rows.label: vv.1-4
rows.text: Woe to the Shepherds / True Shepherds Promised

rows.label: vv.5-6
rows.text: The Righteous Branch: The LORD Our Righteousness

lit.note: Chapter 23 resolves the royal crisis (vv.1-8) and then addresses the prophetic crisis (vv.9-40). The Righteous Branch answers the king problem; the fire/hammer/grain imagery addresses the prophet problem. Both institutions are judged and promised renewal.

hebtext.note: God will raise up "a righteous Branch (ṣemaḥ ṣaddîq) from David's line" (v.5). trans.title: Comparing translations in The Righteous Branch

translations.text: “The days are coming,” declares the Lord, “when I will raise up for David a righteous Branch, a King who will reign wisely and do what is just and right in the land.

“Behold, the days are coming, declares the LORD, when I will raise up for David a righteous Branch, and he shall reign as king and deal wisely, and shall execute justice and righteousness in the land.
“So then, the days are coming,” declares the Lord, “when people will no longer say, ‘As surely as the Lord lives, who brought the Israelites up out of Egypt,’
“Therefore, behold, the days are coming, declares the LORD, when they shall no longer say, ‘As the LORD lives who brought up the people of Israel out of the land of Egypt,’

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: ""Woe to the shepherds who destroy and scatter!" — the indictment names the kings as the primary cause of national catastrophe. The shepherds will be punished for "the evil they have done.""

rec.quote: "Calvin identifies the Righteous Branch as Christ: "No mere human king could bear the name The LORD Our Righteousness. Only the Messiah, who is both God and man, fulfills this prophecy." The pun on Zed…"

Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads The Righteous Branch within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.
